plainMeaning: Padmāvatī, Gaurī, Gāndhārī, Lakṣmaṇā, Susīmā, Jāmbavatī, Satyabhāmā and Rukmiṇī — these are the eight chief queens of Kṛṣṇa, remembered here as great satīs.
recitation: Paümāvaï ya Gorī, Gandhārī Lakhamaṇā Susīmā ya | Jambūvaï Saccabhāmā, Ruppiṇi kaṇha-ha mahisīo

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Padmāvatī, 'she who possesses the lotus,' from padma ('lotus') + possessive suffix -vatī; Prakrit padma → paüma.
wordByWord.notes: In Hemacandra's account hers is the first of the eight queen-houses; the others are placed 'in succession' beside it.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit connective ya, from Skt. ca ('and').
Skt. Gaurī, 'the fair / radiant one,' fem. of gaura ('light-complexioned, brilliant').
Skt. Gāndhārī, 'the lady of Gandhāra,' from the region-name Gandhāra.
wordByWord.notes: Not the Kaurava mother; in the Jain account she is the sister of Cārudatta of Puṣkalāvatī in Gandhāra, married by Kṛṣṇa.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Lakṣmaṇā, 'one marked with auspicious signs,' fem. of lakṣmaṇa, from lakṣman ('mark, sign of fortune'); Prakrit lakkhaṇā / lakhamaṇā.
Skt. Susīmā, 'she of fair bounds,' from su- ('good, fair') + sīmā ('limit, border').
Prakrit connective ya (= Skt. ca), closing the first line's sub-list.
Skt. Jāmbavatī, patronymic 'daughter of Jāmbavat (Jāmbavān),' + fem. -vatī; Prakrit jambū-vaï.
Skt. Satyabhāmā, 'she of true radiance,' satya ('true') + bhāmā ('lustrous lady'); Prakrit sacca = satya.
Skt. Rukmiṇī, 'adorned with gold,' from rukma ('gold'); Prakrit ruppa = rukma.
wordByWord.notes: The name Ruppiṇī also opened the preceding gāthā (v.10); there it stands for a different satī, here specifically for Kṛṣṇa's queen — a deliberate recurrence worth noting.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Kṛṣṇa, 'the dark one'; Prakrit kaṇha. The trailing -ha (here written ઢ/-ḍha) is an Apabhraṃśa-tinged genitive, 'Kṛṣṇa's.'
wordByWord.notes: Other recensions read kaṇhassa ('of Kṛṣṇa'); the book's reading કણ્હઢ is preserved verbatim. Kṛṣṇa here is the Vāsudeva of Jain universal history, cousin of the 22nd Tīrthaṅkara Ariṣṭanemi.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. mahiṣī, 'crowned / chief queen-consort,' fem. of mahiṣa; Prakrit nom. pl. -īo.
wordByWord.notes: The single grammatical head governing all eight preceding names: they are jointly 'the mahiṣīs of Kṛṣṇa.'

literalTranslation: Padmāvatī and Gaurī, Gāndhārī, Lakṣmaṇā and Susīmā; Jāmbavatī, Satyabhāmā, Rukmiṇī — the chief queens of Kṛṣṇa.
idiomaticTranslation: Padmāvatī and Gaurī, Gāndhārī, Lakṣmaṇā and Susīmā, Jāmbavatī, Satyabhāmā, and Rukmiṇī — the eight crowned queens of Kṛṣṇa: these too I remember among the great satīs.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the earlier satīs were each evoked with a story of their own (vv.8–10), this gāthā shifts gear: it names a single royal set — the aṣṭa-mahiṣī, the eight crowned queens of Kṛṣṇa Vāsudeva — bound together by one grammatical head, kaṇha-ha mahisīo ('the queens of Kṛṣṇa'). The roster (Padmāvatī, Gaurī, Gāndhārī, Lakṣmaṇā, Susīmā, Jāmbavatī, Satyabhāmā, Rukmiṇī) matches the canonical Jain list in which these queens later renounce the world under Ariṣṭanemi (Neminātha), Kṛṣṇa's cousin. That is precisely why they belong in a litany of satīs and not in a chronicle of Kṛṣṇa's court: the verse honours them for what they became — renunciants of spotless conduct — not for whose consorts they were. Two small alerts for the careful reciter. First, Gāndhārī here is not the Kaurava matriarch but, in the Jain telling, the sister of Cārudatta of Gandhāra whom Kṛṣṇa won and wed. Second, Ruppiṇī (Rukmiṇī) has already sounded at the head of v.10 for a different woman; its return here, fixed by the genitive 'of Kṛṣṇa,' shows the litany re-using a name without confusion of person. The verse thus rounds off the human, storied satīs by gathering an entire queenly household into the remembrance, and sets up the turn to Sthūlibhadra's seven sisters in v.12.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse-specific teaching is the Jain conviction that worldly station — even queenship in the household of a Vāsudeva — is no bar to liberation, and conversely confers no merit of itself. A Vāsudeva in Jain cosmology is a great but hell-bound worldly hero; his queens are remembered here only because each turned from that splendour to dīkṣā and śīla. Their inclusion makes nāma-grahaṇa do real doctrinal work: the names index vairāgya (dispassion) achieved from the very summit of bhoga (enjoyment), illustrating that detachment, not deprivation, liberates.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this line, one recalls that the highest comforts and the closest tie to a worldly hero did not hold these women back from the path — a quiet check on the reciter's own attachment to status, beauty, and relationship.

crossReferences.text: Antagaḍa-dasāo (Antakṛddaśāḥ), Varga 5 — the renunciation of Kṛṣṇa's eight chief queens under Ariṣṭanemi
Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra, Neminātha-caritra — 'so there were eight chief-queens of Kṛṣṇa, who occupied houses in succession'
crossReferences.source: Hemacandra, tr. Helen M. Johnson (Gaekwad's Oriental Series)

sources.title: Triṣaṣṭiśalākāpuruṣacaritra, Neminātha-caritra — 'Marriage with Gāndhārī' / the eight chief-queens of Kṛṣṇa
sources.consultedFor: Jain identification of Kṛṣṇa's eight chief-queens (including Padmāvatī and Gāndhārī) and Gāndhārī's distinct Jain backstory.

sources.title: Eight chief-queens — Significance in Jainism (Wisdom Library concept page)
sources.consultedFor: Confirmation that 'eight chief-queens' denotes Kṛṣṇa's principal queens in the Jain (Triṣaṣṭi) tradition.

sources.title: Jāmbavatī — definition (Purāṇa Index; Cologne Digital Sanskrit Dictionaries)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and identity of Jāmbavatī as daughter of Jāmbavān and a queen of Kṛṣṇa.

sources.title: Eight queens of Krishna — concept and references
sources.consultedFor: Cross-tradition roster of Kṛṣṇa's principal queens (Rukmiṇī, Satyabhāmā, Jāmbavatī, Lakṣmaṇā) for name verification.

notes: The eight named queens match the canonical Jain aṣṭa-mahiṣī of Kṛṣṇa (Antagaḍa-dasāo, Varga 5; Hemacandra's Neminātha-caritra), all of whom renounce under Ariṣṭanemi — which justifies their place among the satīs. Rosters vary across recensions (some lists give Mūlaśrī/Mūladattā or substitute names); defer to the book's verbatim eight. The token કણ્હઢ (kaṇha-ha/-ḍha) is an unusual genitive form; other recensions read kaṇhassa. The book's spelling is preserved unnormalised. Rukmiṇī (Ruppiṇī) recurs from v.10, where it names a different satī; the genitive 'of Kṛṣṇa' disambiguates the person here.
